title: Design Laws, UX Principles & Heuristics
description: Foundational laws, cognitive principles, Gestalt rules, usability heuristics, and design principles that guide front-end and interaction design.

entries.name: Law of Proximity (Gestalt)
entries.summary: Objects that are near, or proximate to, each other tend to be perceived as belonging to a single group.
entries.significance: Proximity is one of the most powerful and cheapest grouping tools in UI: whitespace alone can communicate relationships without borders, boxes, or color.
key_facts: Formal statement: elements placed close together are perceived as more related than elements placed farther apart.
Originated with the Gestalt psychologists in Germany in the 1920s (Wertheimer, Koffka, Kohler).
UI application: group a form label tightly with its input and add larger gaps between distinct field groups so users read them as separate sections.
Proximity can override similarity of color/shape when the two cues conflict.
examples: Form label sitting close to its field
Related nav links clustered with tight spacing
Card grids using gutters to separate items

entries.name: Law of Similarity (Gestalt)
entries.summary: Elements that share visual characteristics (color, shape, size, orientation) are perceived as related or belonging to the same group.
entries.significance: Similarity lets designers create implicit categories and reinforce consistency, and it underpins why interactive elements should look alike.
key_facts: Formal statement: the human eye perceives similar elements as a complete picture, shape, or group even when they are separated.
A Gestalt principle articulated by Wertheimer, Koffka, and Kohler in the 1920s.
UI application: style all clickable links the same color so users learn one visual pattern for 'this is interactive'.
Breaking similarity deliberately (an odd-colored button) creates emphasis via the Von Restorff effect.
examples: All hyperlinks styled the same blue/underline
Consistent icon style across a toolbar
Matching card shapes signalling one content type

entries.name: Law of Closure (Gestalt)
entries.summary: The mind fills in missing information to perceive a complete, closed figure even when parts of the shape are absent.
entries.significance: Closure explains why minimal, negative-space logos and icons still read as whole objects, letting designers imply form with fewer marks.
key_facts: Formal statement: when we see an incomplete shape, our brain completes the gaps to perceive a recognizable whole.
A core Gestalt principle from the 1920s Berlin School of experimental psychology.
UI application: an icon drawn with broken outlines still reads as the intended object, and loading spinners imply a rotating whole from arcs.
The WWF panda and IBM's striped logo are classic closure examples.

sources.title: The Laws of Figure/Ground, Pragnanz, Closure, and Common Fate

entries.name: Law of Continuity / Good Continuation (Gestalt)
entries.summary: Elements arranged on a line or smooth curve are perceived as related and are followed as a continuous path by the eye.
entries.significance: Continuity is why alignment matters: items on a shared axis feel connected, guiding the eye along intended reading and scanning paths.
key_facts: Formal statement: the eye follows the smoothest path and perceives elements on a continuous line/curve as a single group.
A Gestalt principle from Wertheimer's early 20th-century work on perception.
UI application: aligning menu items or a breadcrumb trail on one axis makes them read as a connected sequence.
Interruptions in a continuous line draw attention because they break the expected flow.

entries.name: Law of Common Region (Gestalt)
entries.summary: Elements enclosed within a shared boundary or background are perceived as belonging to the same group.
entries.significance: Common region is the perceptual basis for cards, panels, and fieldsets: a border or background color binds content into a unit even without proximity.
key_facts: Formal statement: elements tend to be perceived into groups if they share an area with a clearly defined boundary.
Added to Gestalt theory later by Stephen Palmer (1992).
UI application: wrapping a heading, image, and button in a card with a shared background makes them read as one clickable unit.
Common region can override proximity, grouping distant items that share an enclosure.

entries.name: Figure/Ground (Gestalt)
entries.summary: The eye separates a visual scene into a foreground figure that holds attention and a background ground that recedes.
entries.significance: Figure/ground is fundamental to establishing hierarchy and modality: modals, dropdowns, and overlays rely on it to pull the active layer forward.
key_facts: Formal statement: we instinctively perceive objects as either the figure (the focus) or the ground (the surrounding backdrop).
A Gestalt principle; Edgar Rubin's vase illustration (1915) is the classic ambiguous figure/ground demonstration.
UI application: dimming the page behind a modal (a scrim/overlay) pushes the dialog forward as the figure.
Strong contrast, shadow, and blur reinforce which layer is the figure.

sources.title: The Laws of Figure/Ground, Pragnanz, Closure, and Common Fate

entries.name: Law of Symmetry (Gestalt)
entries.summary: Symmetrical elements are perceived as belonging together and as forming a coherent, stable whole, regardless of their distance.
entries.significance: Symmetry conveys order, balance, and trustworthiness, which is why formal and professional interfaces lean on symmetrical layouts.
key_facts: Formal statement: the mind perceives objects as symmetrical and arranged around a center point, seeking balance.
A Gestalt principle rooted in the 1920s Berlin School.
UI application: symmetrical arrangement of paired buttons or mirrored columns feels resolved and intentional.
Symmetry competes with proximity; the brain prefers to group symmetrical pairs even when unequal spacing suggests otherwise.

entries.name: Law of Common Fate (Gestalt)
entries.summary: Elements that move in the same direction, or change together, are perceived as belonging to the same group.
entries.significance: Common fate is the only Gestalt principle rooted in motion, making it central to animation, parallax, and coordinated transitions in modern UIs.
key_facts: Formal statement: elements moving together (same direction, speed, or timing) are perceived as a unit.
A Gestalt principle; motion is a stronger grouping cue than static similarity or proximity.
UI application: list items that slide in together on load are read as one set; a parallax layer moving as a whole reads as one plane.
Coordinated micro-interactions (a group of icons animating in sync) signal that they are functionally related.

sources.title: The Laws of Figure/Ground, Pragnanz, Closure, and Common Fate

entries.name: Law of Pragnanz (Law of Good Figure / Simplicity)
entries.summary: People perceive and interpret ambiguous or complex images in the simplest form possible, because simpler interpretations require less cognitive effort.
entries.significance: Pragnanz is the overarching Gestalt law and a scientific rationale for minimalism: reduce complexity and the brain grasps the design faster.
key_facts: Formal statement: we tend to order our experience in a manner that is regular, orderly, symmetrical, and simple (Pragnanz means 'conciseness' in German).
Considered the fundamental principle of Gestalt perception, from Wertheimer's work.
UI application: reducing an interface to essential shapes and clear structure lets users perceive it as one coherent whole rather than scattered parts.
The Olympic rings are perceived as five simple overlapping circles rather than a set of complex intersecting arcs.

entries.name: Law of Uniform Connectedness (Gestalt)
entries.summary: Elements that are visually connected by a line, color, frame, or other shared region are perceived as more related than elements with no connection.
entries.significance: Uniform connectedness is the strongest grouping principle and the basis for connectors, dividers, and linked steps in wizards and flowcharts.
key_facts: Formal statement: elements connected to each other by uniform visual properties are perceived as a single group.
Added to Gestalt theory by Stephen Palmer and Irvin Rock (1994).
UI application: a connecting line between steps in a multi-step progress bar makes them read as one linked sequence.
It generally overrides proximity and similarity when connections are explicit.

entries.name: Fitts's Law
entries.summary: The time to acquire a target is a function of the distance to and the size of the target: closer and larger targets are faster to hit.
entries.significance: Fitts's Law is the quantitative foundation for button sizing, edge/corner placement, and touch-target guidance across every pointing interface.
key_facts: Formal statement: the time to acquire a target is a function of the distance to and size of the target (larger/closer = faster).
Derived by psychologist Paul Fitts in 1954 from experiments on human motor movement.
UI application: make primary buttons large and place frequent actions near the cursor or along screen edges (edges/corners are effectively infinite-width targets).
It directly motivates minimum touch-target sizes such as WCAG 2.5.5's 44x44 CSS pixels.
examples: macOS menu bar pinned to the screen edge
Right-click context menus that appear at the cursor

entries.name: Hick's Law (Hick-Hyman Law)
entries.summary: The time it takes to make a decision increases logarithmically with the number and complexity of choices available.
entries.significance: Hick's Law justifies reducing options, chunking menus, and using progressive disclosure to keep decisions fast and painless.
key_facts: Formal statement: decision time increases logarithmically with the number of choices presented.
Named after psychologists William Edmund Hick and Ray Hyman, who studied it in 1952.
UI application: break long menus into categories, highlight recommended options, and hide advanced settings behind progressive disclosure.
It does not mean always minimize choices; simplifying can hide complexity that users still need (see Tesler's Law).
examples: Curated 'recommended' plan on a pricing page

entries.name: Jakob's Law
entries.summary: Users spend most of their time on other sites, so they prefer your site to work the same way as the sites they already know.
entries.significance: Jakob's Law is the argument for following established conventions rather than reinventing patterns: familiarity lowers the learning curve.
key_facts: Formal statement: users transfer expectations built around one familiar product to another that appears similar.
Coined by usability expert Jakob Nielsen (co-founder of Nielsen Norman Group).
UI application: put the logo top-left linking home, the cart top-right, and search where users expect them, matching existing mental models.
Departing from convention is possible but must be justified by clearly superior value, since it costs users relearning.
examples: Shopping cart icon in the top-right

entries.name: Miller's Law (7 plus or minus 2)
entries.summary: The average person can hold about seven (plus or minus two) items in their working memory at once.
entries.significance: Miller's Law underpins chunking: grouping information into digestible units reduces cognitive load, though it is often misapplied as a hard 'menus must have 7 items' rule.
key_facts: Formal statement: the average person can keep only about 7 (plus or minus 2) items in working memory.
From cognitive psychologist George A. Miller's 1956 paper 'The Magical Number Seven, Plus or Minus Two'.
UI application: chunk long strings like phone numbers and card numbers into groups (e.g., 555-123-4567) to ease recall.
Common misinterpretation: it is NOT a mandate to limit navigation to 7 items; it is about working memory, not menu length.
examples: Credit card fields split into 4-digit chunks

entries.name: Doherty Threshold
entries.summary: Productivity and engagement soar when a computer and its user interact at a pace (under 400 ms) that neither has to wait on the other.
entries.significance: The Doherty Threshold sets a concrete performance target and justifies optimistic UI, skeleton screens, and progress feedback to mask latency.
key_facts: Formal statement: system response times under 400 milliseconds keep users engaged and feeling in flow.
Established by Walter J. Doherty and Ahrvind J. Thadani at IBM in 1982.
UI application: use skeleton loaders, optimistic updates, and progress indicators so perceived response stays under the threshold even when work is slow.
Perceived performance can matter more than actual speed; animation and feedback buy tolerance for real delays.

entries.name: Aesthetic-Usability Effect
entries.summary: Users often perceive aesthetically pleasing designs as more usable, even when they are not objectively easier to use.
entries.significance: This effect explains why visual polish builds trust and tolerance for minor usability flaws, but also warns that beauty can mask real problems in testing.
key_facts: Formal statement: users perceive attractive products as more usable and are more forgiving of their usability issues.
Documented by Masaaki Kurosu and Kaori Kashimura in a 1995 study at the Hitachi Design Center.
UI application: invest in visual craft to increase perceived quality and trust, especially on first impressions and onboarding.
Caution: aesthetic appeal can hide usability problems from stakeholders and mislead usability tests.
examples: Polished onboarding screens increasing perceived ease
Attractive apps forgiven for small friction

entries.name: Von Restorff Effect (Isolation Effect)
entries.summary: When multiple similar objects are present, the one that differs from the rest is the most likely to be remembered.
entries.significance: The isolation effect is the psychology behind visual hierarchy: making the primary action distinct ensures it is noticed and recalled.
key_facts: Formal statement: an item that stands out from its peers is more likely to be noticed and remembered.
Named after psychiatrist and pediatrician Hedwig von Restorff, who identified it in 1933.
UI application: give the primary button a unique high-contrast color so it stands apart from secondary actions.
Do not rely on color alone to isolate an element; also use size, weight, or position for accessibility.

entries.name: Serial Position Effect
entries.summary: Users best remember the first and last items in a series (primacy and recency), and are worse at recalling those in the middle.
entries.significance: It guides how to order navigation and lists: place the most important items at the start and end where they are most likely to be remembered.
key_facts: Formal statement: items at the beginning (primacy) and end (recency) of a list are recalled better than middle items.
The term traces to psychologist Hermann Ebbinghaus's memory research in the late 1800s.
UI application: put the highest-priority nav links first and last, and place key actions at the top or bottom of menus.
Combines with Von Restorff: an emphasized middle item can overcome its weak recall position.
examples: Home first and account/logout last in a nav
Most-used tools at the ends of a toolbar

entries.name: Peak-End Rule
entries.summary: People judge an experience largely based on how they felt at its most intense point (the peak) and at its end, rather than the average of every moment.
entries.significance: It directs design effort toward emotional high points and endings of a journey, where impressions are cemented, rather than spreading polish evenly.
key_facts: Formal statement: an experience is judged largely on its most intense moment and its final moment.
Identified by Daniel Kahneman and Barbara Fredrickson through behavioral-economics research.
UI application: craft delightful success/confirmation screens and smooth endings, and soften painful peaks like errors.
A strong ending can compensate for friction earlier in the flow.

entries.name: Zeigarnik Effect
entries.summary: People remember uncompleted or interrupted tasks better than completed ones, and feel a pull to finish what they started.
entries.significance: It powers progress indicators, onboarding checklists, and completion meters that motivate users to finish tasks.
key_facts: Formal statement: unfinished tasks create psychological tension that keeps them in memory until completed.
Discovered by Soviet psychologist Bluma Zeigarnik in the 1920s after observing waiters recall unpaid orders better.
UI application: show a profile-completion bar or onboarding checklist to nudge users toward finishing setup.
Displaying an already-partially-filled progress bar (endowed progress) increases completion rates.

entries.name: Tesler's Law (Law of Conservation of Complexity)
entries.summary: For any system there is a certain amount of complexity that cannot be reduced; it can only be shifted between the product and its users.
entries.significance: Tesler's Law reminds designers that simplifying the interface often means the product must absorb the complexity internally, rather than pushing it onto users.
key_facts: Formal statement: every application has an inherent amount of irreducible complexity; the only question is who deals with it.
Coined by Larry Tesler, a computer scientist who worked at Xerox PARC and Apple, in the mid-1980s.
UI application: auto-detect a credit-card type from its number so the system handles the complexity instead of asking the user.
Over-simplifying the UI can dump hidden complexity back onto users; the goal is to move it to where it is best handled.
examples: Email clients guessing the intended recipient

entries.name: Postel's Law (Robustness Principle)
entries.summary: Be conservative in what you send, be liberal in what you accept: design systems to tolerate varied and imperfect user input.
entries.significance: Postel's Law argues for forgiving inputs (flexible formats, error tolerance) so users are not punished for minor deviations.
key_facts: Formal statement: be liberal in what you accept, and conservative in what you send.
Formulated by computer scientist Jon Postel in 1980 in the TCP specification (RFC 761).
UI application: accept phone numbers with or without dashes/spaces and normalize them behind the scenes instead of rejecting them.
Applied to UX, it means anticipating messy human input and handling it gracefully rather than demanding rigid formats.
examples: Phone/date fields that accept multiple formats

entries.name: Occam's Razor
entries.summary: Among competing solutions that predict equally well, the one with the fewest assumptions should be selected; the simplest solution tends to be the best.
entries.significance: Occam's Razor is a bias toward simplicity: strip away unnecessary elements and steps until only what is essential remains.
key_facts: Formal statement: entities should not be multiplied without necessity; prefer the simplest adequate solution.
Attributed to 14th-century Franciscan friar and philosopher William of Ockham.
UI application: remove non-essential form fields, decorations, and steps that do not serve the user's goal.
Simplicity here means fewest necessary parts, not fewest features at the cost of usefulness.

entries.name: Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)
entries.summary: Roughly 80% of effects come from 20% of the causes; a small share of features typically drives the majority of usage and value.
entries.significance: The Pareto Principle guides prioritization: focus design and optimization effort on the vital few features and flows that most users rely on.
key_facts: Formal statement: for many outcomes, roughly 80% of consequences come from 20% of causes.
Named after economist Vilfredo Pareto, who observed in 1896 that 80% of Italy's land was owned by 20% of the population.
UI application: identify the 20% of features that drive 80% of engagement and make those the most prominent and polished.
The exact ratio varies; the principle is about disproportionate impact, not a literal 80/20 split.
examples: Optimizing the most common user journey first

entries.name: Parkinson's Law
entries.summary: Work expands so as to fill the time available for its completion; tasks take as long as the time allotted to them.
entries.significance: In UX, it motivates imposing helpful constraints (deadlines, autofill, streamlined flows) so users complete tasks faster than they otherwise would.
key_facts: Formal statement: work expands to fill the time available for its completion.
Coined by naval historian Cyril Northcote Parkinson in a 1955 essay in The Economist.
UI application: reduce the perceived and actual time a task takes with autofill, smart defaults, and progress limits so users finish quickly.
Time constraints and streamlined flows can prevent tasks from ballooning in effort.

entries.name: Goal-Gradient Effect
entries.summary: The tendency to approach a goal increases with proximity to it: people accelerate their effort as they get closer to completion.
entries.significance: It explains why progress bars and 'you're almost there' cues boost completion, and why endowed early progress increases motivation.
key_facts: Formal statement: motivation to reach a goal increases as one gets nearer to achieving it.
Rooted in Clark Hull's 1930s behavioral research and revisited by Kivetz et al. (2006) in a loyalty-program study.
UI application: show a progress bar that advances toward completion and grant some initial progress to trigger the effect earlier.
Artificially starting a loyalty card at 2/12 punches (endowed progress) drives faster completion than starting at 0/10.
examples: Loyalty punch cards with a head start

entries.name: Visibility of System Status (Nielsen Heuristic 1)
entries.summary: The design should always keep users informed about what is going on, through appropriate, timely feedback.
entries.significance: The first of Nielsen's 10 heuristics; visible status builds trust and helps users make good decisions based on the current state.
key_facts: Formal statement: keep users informed about what is happening through appropriate feedback within a reasonable time.
Part of Jakob Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics, first published in 1994 (refined from 1990 work with Rolf Molich).
UI application: show loading spinners, upload progress, saved/unsaved indicators, and current step in a checkout.
Closely tied to the Doherty Threshold: timely feedback keeps users engaged.

sources.title: 10 Usability Heuristics for User Interface Design

entries.name: Match Between System and the Real World (Nielsen Heuristic 2)
entries.summary: The design should speak the users' language, using familiar words, phrases, and concepts rather than internal jargon, and follow real-world conventions.
entries.significance: Speaking the user's language and following real-world order makes information feel natural and reduces the effort of interpretation.
key_facts: Formal statement: the system should speak the users' language and make information appear in a natural, logical order.
The second of Jakob Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics (1994).
UI application: use plain labels like 'Trash' or 'Cart' and real-world metaphors instead of database/engineering terms.
Related to Jakob's Law: conventions and familiar mental models reduce learning cost.

sources.title: 10 Usability Heuristics for User Interface Design

entries.name: User Control and Freedom (Nielsen Heuristic 3)
entries.summary: Users need a clearly marked emergency exit to leave unwanted states, with support for undo and redo.
entries.significance: Providing exits and undo reduces the fear of making mistakes and encourages exploration.
key_facts: Formal statement: users often perform actions by mistake and need a clearly marked way to undo or exit without an extended process.
The third of Jakob Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics (1994).
UI application: provide undo/redo, cancel buttons, and easy dismissal of dialogs and modals.
Supports error recovery and lowers the stakes of experimentation.

sources.title: 10 Usability Heuristics for User Interface Design

entries.name: Consistency and Standards (Nielsen Heuristic 4)
entries.summary: Users should not have to wonder whether different words, situations, or actions mean the same thing; follow platform and industry conventions.
entries.significance: Internal and external consistency reduces cognitive load and leverages knowledge users bring from other products.
key_facts: Formal statement: follow platform and industry conventions (external consistency) and keep terms/actions uniform within the product (internal consistency).
The fourth of Jakob Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics (1994).
UI application: use the same label, icon, and behavior for the same action everywhere in the app.
Directly reinforced by Jakob's Law and design systems.
examples: Uniform button styles across an app

sources.title: 10 Usability Heuristics for User Interface Design

entries.name: Error Prevention (Nielsen Heuristic 5)
entries.summary: Good error messages are important, but the best designs prevent problems from occurring in the first place.
entries.significance: Preventing errors is more effective than handling them; eliminate error-prone conditions or confirm risky actions before they happen.
key_facts: Formal statement: either eliminate error-prone conditions or check for them and present a confirmation before users commit.
The fifth of Jakob Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics (1994).
UI application: disable invalid options, use input constraints/date pickers, and confirm destructive actions like delete.
Distinguishes slips (unconscious errors) from mistakes (conscious wrong decisions); both should be designed against.

sources.title: 10 Usability Heuristics for User Interface Design

entries.name: Recognition Rather Than Recall (Nielsen Heuristic 6)
entries.summary: Minimize memory load by making elements, actions, and options visible so users recognize information rather than having to recall it.
entries.significance: Recognition is easier than recall; surfacing choices and context reduces cognitive burden and errors.
key_facts: Formal statement: make objects, actions, and options visible so users don't have to remember information from one part of the interface to another.
The sixth of Jakob Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics (1994).
UI application: show recently viewed items, autocomplete suggestions, and visible menu options instead of memorized commands.
Relies on Miller's Law: reducing working-memory load prevents overload.

sources.title: 10 Usability Heuristics for User Interface Design

entries.name: Flexibility and Efficiency of Use (Nielsen Heuristic 7)
entries.summary: Shortcuts and accelerators, hidden from novices, can speed up interaction for expert users so the design serves both.
entries.significance: Catering to both novice and expert users via accelerators lets the product scale with a user's growing proficiency.
key_facts: Formal statement: accelerators unseen by novice users may speed up interaction for experts, letting the design serve both inexperienced and experienced users.
The seventh of Jakob Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics (1994).
UI application: offer keyboard shortcuts, saved templates, and customizable dashboards alongside discoverable menus.
Personalization and customization are forms of flexibility that reward frequent users.

sources.title: 10 Usability Heuristics for User Interface Design

entries.name: Aesthetic and Minimalist Design (Nielsen Heuristic 8)
entries.summary: Interfaces should not contain information that is irrelevant or rarely needed; every extra unit of content competes with the relevant units.
entries.significance: Keeping content focused improves the signal-to-noise ratio, so essential information is not diluted by clutter.
key_facts: Formal statement: interfaces should not contain information which is irrelevant or rarely needed, as it diminishes the relative visibility of what matters.
The eighth of Jakob Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics (1994).
UI application: cut decorative clutter, keep primary content prominent, and defer secondary details via progressive disclosure.
Minimalist does not mean flat or featureless; it means keeping only what serves the user's goal.

sources.title: 10 Usability Heuristics for User Interface Design

entries.name: Help Users Recognize, Diagnose, and Recover from Errors (Nielsen Heuristic 9)
entries.summary: Error messages should be expressed in plain language, precisely indicate the problem, and constructively suggest a solution.
entries.significance: Clear, actionable error recovery turns frustrating dead-ends into recoverable moments and reduces abandonment.
key_facts: Formal statement: error messages should be in plain language (no codes), precisely indicate the problem, and constructively suggest a solution.
The ninth of Jakob Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics (1994).
UI application: 'Enter a valid email like name@example.com' instead of 'Error 422'; highlight the offending field.
Traditionally presented with visual treatment (bold, red) that clearly marks the error and how to fix it.
examples: 404 pages with search and links home

sources.title: 10 Usability Heuristics for User Interface Design

entries.name: Help and Documentation (Nielsen Heuristic 10)
entries.summary: It is best if the system needs no documentation, but it may be necessary to provide help that is easy to search, task-focused, and concrete.
entries.significance: Well-placed, task-focused help supports users at their moment of need without forcing them to leave the flow.
key_facts: Formal statement: provide help and documentation that is easy to search, focused on the user's task, lists concrete steps, and isn't too large.
The tenth of Jakob Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics (1994).
UI application: contextual tooltips, inline hints, and searchable help centers placed where users get stuck.
The ideal is an interface so intuitive that documentation is rarely needed.

sources.title: 10 Usability Heuristics for User Interface Design

entries.name: C.R.A.P. Principles (Contrast, Repetition, Alignment, Proximity)
entries.summary: Four foundational graphic-design principles - Contrast, Repetition, Alignment, and Proximity - for creating clear, organized, and attractive layouts.
entries.significance: C.R.A.P. distills visual design into four memorable, teachable rules that apply to any layout from documents to web pages.
key_facts: The four principles are Contrast (make different things clearly different), Repetition (reuse visual elements for unity), Alignment (place elements on shared axes), and Proximity (group related items).
Introduced by Robin Williams in 'The Non-Designer's Design Book', first published in 1994.
UI application: contrast a bold heading against body text, repeat button styles, align form fields to a grid, and group related controls.
Two of the four (Proximity, and Contrast/Alignment) overlap directly with Gestalt principles.

sources.title: How to Use C.R.A.P. Design Principles For Better UX

entries.name: Dieter Rams' Ten Principles for Good Design
entries.summary: Ten timeless criteria articulated by designer Dieter Rams describing what makes design good, culminating in 'good design is as little design as possible'.
entries.significance: Rams' principles shaped modern product and interface design (notably influencing Apple's Jony Ive) and are a benchmark for minimalist, honest design.
key_facts: The ten: good design is innovative; useful; aesthetic; makes a product understandable; unobtrusive; honest; long-lasting; thorough down to the last detail; environmentally friendly; and as little design as possible.
Written by Dieter Rams in the late 1970s while he was head of design at Braun; he later designed furniture for Vitsoe.
His guiding maxim is 'Weniger, aber besser' ('Less, but better').
UI application: strive for interfaces that are useful, honest about their function, understandable, and free of decoration for its own sake.

sources.title: Dieter Rams: 10 Timeless Commandments for Good Design

entries.name: Don't Make Me Think (Krug's Law of Usability)
entries.summary: A web page or app should be self-evident, obvious, and self-explanatory so users can understand what to do without expending mental effort.
entries.significance: Steve Krug's first law reframes usability around minimizing cognitive effort: if users have to think about how the interface works, the design has failed.
key_facts: Krug's first law of usability: 'Don't make me think.'
From Steve Krug's book 'Don't Make Me Think', first published in 2000 (revised 2014).
UI application: use obvious clickable affordances, clear labels, and self-explanatory navigation so nothing requires deciphering.
Related to the concept that every question mark a user forms adds to their cognitive workload.

entries.name: Progressive Disclosure
entries.summary: An interaction-design technique that defers advanced or rarely used features to a secondary screen, showing only essential options first.
entries.significance: Progressive disclosure manages complexity by revealing detail on demand, keeping the initial experience simple while still supporting power features.
key_facts: Definition: show only the necessary or commonly used options up front, and reveal additional options when the user asks for them.
Popularized in HCI by Jakob Nielsen and rooted in cognitive-load theory.
UI application: an 'Advanced settings' expander, 'Show more' links, and multi-step wizards that reveal complexity gradually.
Reduces both the perceived and actual complexity users face at any one moment (supports Hick's Law and Tesler's Law).

entries.name: 8-Point Grid System
entries.summary: A spacing methodology where all sizing and spacing values are multiples of 8 pixels (with 4px as a half-step) to create visual rhythm and consistency.
entries.significance: The 8pt grid gives teams a shared, scalable spacing language that keeps layouts consistent and maps cleanly onto common screen densities.
key_facts: Rule: use multiples of 8 (8, 16, 24, 32, 48...) for margins, padding, and dimensions, often with 4px as a permitted half-step.
Popularized through Google's Material Design, which specifies an 8dp baseline grid for layout.
Rationale: many popular screen resolutions are divisible by 8, so 8-based values scale to whole pixels across densities and reduce blurry rendering.
UI application: define spacing tokens (--space-1: 8px, --space-2: 16px...) so designers and developers share one scale.
examples: Spacing token scales in design systems

entries.name: Affordances (Norman)
entries.summary: An affordance is a relationship between an object's properties and a user's capabilities that determines how the object could possibly be used.
entries.significance: Affordances explain why some controls feel obviously usable; designing perceivable affordances is core to intuitive interfaces.
key_facts: Definition (Norman): an affordance is the relationship between the properties of an object and the capabilities of the agent that determine how it could be used.
The term was coined by psychologist James J. Gibson; Don Norman adapted it for design in 'The Design of Everyday Things' (1988).
UI application: a raised, shadowed button affords pressing; a text field affords typing.
Norman later clarified that on screens we mostly design perceivable signifiers of affordances, not affordances themselves.

entries.name: Signifiers (Norman)
entries.summary: Signifiers are perceivable cues that communicate where and how an action should take place, making affordances discoverable.
entries.significance: In screen-based design, signifiers do the real work of discoverability, since users cannot feel digital affordances directly.
key_facts: Definition: a signifier is any perceivable indicator that communicates appropriate behavior to a user.
Introduced by Don Norman in the revised edition of 'The Design of Everyday Things' (2013) to sharpen the loose use of 'affordance'.
UI application: underlined link text, a cursor changing to a pointer, and 'drag here' labels are signifiers that reveal what to do.
Norman's rule of thumb: affordances determine what actions are possible; signifiers communicate where the action should take place.

entries.name: Mapping (Norman)
entries.summary: Mapping is the relationship between controls and their effects; good (natural) mapping uses spatial and conceptual analogies so the layout of controls matches the layout of what they affect.
entries.significance: Natural mapping lets users predict outcomes without instructions, reducing errors and learning time.
key_facts: Definition: mapping describes the correspondence between controls and the things they control.
A core concept in Don Norman's 'The Design of Everyday Things'; his classic example is a stove whose burner controls mirror the burner layout.
UI application: arrange volume up above volume down, and place a settings panel's controls in the spatial order of what they change.
Poor mapping (arbitrary control-to-effect relationships) is a common source of user error.

sources.title: The Design of Everyday Things - Principles

entries.name: Feedback (Norman)
entries.summary: Feedback is the communication of the results of an action, letting the user know the system received and is processing their input.
entries.significance: Immediate, informative feedback closes the loop between action and result, which is essential to a sense of control and correctness.
key_facts: Definition: feedback communicates the results of an action back to the user, and must be immediate and informative.
A central principle in Don Norman's 'The Design of Everyday Things'; poor or absent feedback leaves users uncertain.
UI application: button press states, hover changes, loading spinners, and success/error toasts all provide feedback.
Norman warns that too much feedback (over-notification) can be as harmful as too little.
examples: Haptic buzz on a mobile action

sources.title: The Design of Everyday Things - Principles

entries.name: Constraints (Norman)
entries.summary: Constraints are limitations on possible actions that guide users toward correct behavior and prevent errors.
entries.significance: Constraints simplify decisions and prevent mistakes by making incorrect actions difficult or impossible.
key_facts: Definition: constraints restrict the set of possible actions, narrowing choices to appropriate ones.
Don Norman describes four types in 'The Design of Everyday Things': physical, cultural, semantic, and logical constraints.
UI application: disable a submit button until required fields are valid, or grey out unavailable options.
Constraints work hand-in-hand with error prevention by removing the opportunity for invalid actions.

sources.title: The Design of Everyday Things - Principles
